metadata.title: What Is “Rural Feminism”? A Computer-Assisted Analysis of Popular Antifeminist Discourses in Chinese Social Media

abstract: Along with the popularization of feminism in Chinese social media, an antifeminist wave has become increasingly prominent since 2014, marked most notably by the stigmatization buzzword "rural feminism." We collected 2,104 texts regarding "rural feminism" on Zhihu (the Chinese Quora) and applied approaches of computer-assisted discourse studies (i.e., topic modeling, collocation, and concordance analyses) as lenses to help us understand the connections and patterns within these fragmented social media texts, to uncover hidden antifeminist strategies. The findings reveal that popular antifeminism adopts strategies that we defined as "double embrace" and "double rejection" to minimize the realistic threat of feminism to men's vested interests. These deliberate strategies hint at and further exacerbate the complex interplay between current Chinese antifeminism and feminism. On this basis, we argue that the previous framework of understanding this relationship as a simple dichotomy no longer applies and that we need to reconceptualize the current challenges and threats to Chinese feminism in the context of the specific history and reality of Chinese society and the rich interactions between antifeminism and misogyny, neoliberalism, postfeminism, and various versions of feminism active in China.

sections.paper_id: Yang et al. - 2023 - What Is “Rural Feminism” A Computer-Assisted Analysis of Popular Antifeminist Discourses in Chinese
paragraphs: Since 2014, feminists all over the world have progressively utilized new technologies, especially social media platforms, to confront "contemporary sexism, misogyny, and rape culture," following an increasing intention of the public to resist and challenge social inequalities inclusive of sexism and patriarchy via feminist uptake digital communication (Mendes et al., 2018, pp. 236-237). The trending of the #MeToo hashtag on Twitter is a typical case of combining the feminist movement with social media. In China, studies have shown the rising visibility of feminism in Chinese popular and media discourse (e.g., Huang, 2016;Chang et al., 2018;Han, 2018;Wu & Dong, 2019;X. Yang et al., 2022), and Chinese feminists are also skilled at using social media to attract public attention to issues regarding women and gender equality (Wang & Driscoll, 2019).

Along with the prevalence of feminism, the backlash against it is becoming more and more pronounced on Chinese social media (e.g., Cao & Xu, 2015;Hong, 2016). Initially, the antifeminist discourses present online caught our attention with a buzzword, "rural feminism" (tian yuan nv quan), which is often used to derogate feminist ideas and practices. This internet buzzword, although mentioned in a series of academic studies on Chinese feminism, is usually introduced only briefly as an example of the stigmatization of feminism in Chinese online discourse (e.g., Wu & Dong, 2019;Li & Gao, 2020;Yin, 2022), while the few studies that focused on rural feminism have tended to take it as a corrective to feminism rather than associating it with the increasingly pronounced backlash against feminism (e.g., Du, 2017;Guo, 2019). Research into the specific mechanisms through which rural feminism may stigmatize and undermine Chinese feminism has so far been lacking. Exactly this lack of in-depth research may play an important role in the polarized view of this buzzword.

Furthermore, we identified a main Chinese social media platform, Zhihu (the Chinese Quora), as the base of antifeminist discourses. Launched in January 2010 by Yuan Zhou, Zhihu nowadays has developed into the largest Q&Ainspired online community and counts among the largest comprehensive online content communities in China. As of 31 March 2022, its total assets amounted to US$1,325,850,000. 1 Given Zhihu's size and influence, the popular topics and discourse on this platform are of great value in understanding the overall picture of current Chinese online discourse and social ideologies. A very noteworthy phenomenon in the rich discussions on Zhihu is the scale and popularity of antifeminist discourse, which is not only confirmed by previous research (e.g., Ma, 2020), but also supported by the popularity of antifeminism topics on the platform. On 30 January 2022, "Antifeminism" 2 is the second most popular feminist topic on Zhihu. And the most popular topic, "Feminist Thinking," 3 also shows an overwhelmingly negative attitude toward feminism, with a special focus on undesirable images of feminists. Zhihu is, therefore, generally recognized as a stronghold of antifeminist rhetoric, and a great window into China's online antifeminist wave. In this vein, taking "rural feminism" as the entry point and Zhihu as the site for investigation, we inspected the discourses of online antifeminism prevalent in Chinese social media.

As demonstrated in well-established research (e.g., Wu & Dong, 2019;Gan, 2021), gender antagonism is a long-standing and systematic social problem in Chinese society, and the discursive landscape based on gender conflict takes on an extremely varied and complex form. This study attempts to approach this domain from a relatively microscopic, empirical, and practical perspective. Specifically, we hope to contribute to the understanding of gender antagonism in China, in particular of its discourse domain, in two ways. First, based on a large amount of textual data, we describe and summarize the main strategies manifested in the antifeminist discourses created around the buzzword of rural feminism on Chinese social media today, and explore the means that these discourses have adopted to stigmatize feminist ideas and practices. By comprehensively summarizing the strategies of this backlash against feminism, we hope to provide an empirical basis for the exploration and theorization of liberation politics of gender in China today.

Our second objective is to verify whether the computerassisted discourse analysis method employed herein is valid and effective for identifying the connections and patterns in large-scale fragmented texts. The current information environment, which is highly dependent on social media, not only offers new ways of exercising discursive power, but also poses sharp methodological challenges to discourse studies, the most pressing of which is the vast amount of unstructured textual data commonly associated with social media (Törnberg & Törnberg, 2016). Furthermore, there are two major and interconnected criticisms that long accompanied discourse analysis-the limited number of texts (e.g., Stubbs, 1994) and the random selection of the texts (e.g., Koller & Mautner, 2004), both of which limit to some extent the representativeness of the texts and the validity of the conclusions. And these issues become more pronounced in the face of social media texts. Therefore, we adopted approaches of computer-assisted discourse studies (i.e., topic modeling, collocation, and concordance analyses) to process the large number of texts collected from Zhihu. In this vein, the burgeoning literature on the dynamics of the discursive landscape of gender-based struggles in public discourse, as well as the underpinned gendered power relations, forms an appropriate foundation and context for this study. It furthermore offers invaluable resources to cross-validate the effectiveness of the methodological synergy used herein for in-depth analysis of large volumes of unstructured texts.

sections.paper_id: Yang et al. - 2023 - What Is “Rural Feminism” A Computer-Assisted Analysis of Popular Antifeminist Discourses in Chinese
Yang et al. - 2023 - What Is “Rural Feminism” A Computer-Assisted Analysis of Popular Antifeminist Discourses in Chinese
paragraphs: Since viewing gender relations as relations of power is a central tenet of feminism, in a sense, changes in women's power inevitably lead to changes in men's power, and any advancement in women's status must be accompanied by a relative decline in men's status (Segal, 1990). Therefore, "every large-scale, organized attempt by women to advance their status in society has been met with resistance" (Ging & Siapera, 2019, p. 3). The magnitude and intensity of antifeminist sentiment on the internet globally has reached such a dramatic level since 2011 that it has become a serious concern for feminism. In addition to targeting feminist activists, this adverse sentiment and behavior are directed indiscriminately at any woman who expresses an opinion or exerts influence in the digital space, whether or not she self-identifies as a feminist (e.g., Jane, 2016;Cockerill, 2019;Ging & Siapera, 2019).

The mass hatred of women is not a recent invention, but among the oldest prejudices under a familiar name-misogyny (Holland, 2006). With the development of internet technology, misogyny that has long existed in the social sense has also diffused on social media platforms. As argued by Banet-Weiser and Miltner (2016), this networked misogyny marks the dawn of a new era of gender warfare. This networked misogyny, despite its dependence on a new medium, is in line with the traditional prescriptions of misogyny that have always existed in gender political ideologies, which include (1) the insistence that women are inferior; (2) that the primary value of women lies in their sexual function; (3) that women do not belong in the public sphere; and (4) that those women who cross the line must retreat to their original position or they should be punished (Gan, 2021).

While there are important distinctions between misogyny and antifeminism-the former is often understood as a generally hostile set of attitudes and behaviors toward women; the latter implies opposition to feminism as a distinct set of gendered political values not exclusively espoused by women-it is now clear that we are seeing an increasing blurring of this boundary (Ging & Siapera, 2019). Online antifeminism now, unlike previous offline movements, exhibits extreme misogyny and aggression toward individual women (Ging, 2019).

On Chinese social media, amid a growing gender antagonism, the target of antifeminist attacks has increasingly moved from abstract gender political values to concrete female individuals, so that the line between antifeminism and misogyny has almost disappeared. This phenomenon is clearly demonstrated in the discussion related to rural feminism-the buzzword was popularized in the context of sparring among social media users, and it has been commonly used from the beginning to attack specific female users who made comments, which exhibits clear signs of misogyny. This attack on specific individuals also makes the term more threatening and hurtful, making other women more afraid to speak out publicly about dissent. It is also important to note that the anonymity provided by computer-mediated communication, especially via social media, gives a sense of impunity and can lead to a loss of self-awareness and empathy (Barlow & Awan, 2016). As a result, semiotic attacks against specific women are further enabled. And the tolerance and silence on the part of women in response to denigrating remarks is essentially also a manifestation of misogyny. In this context, the popularity of the term rural feminism signifies not only the prevalence of antifeminist trends, but also the proliferation of misogyny in current Chinese society. Consistent with the findings of many studies in other contexts, Chinese social media platforms present both opportunities and challenges for women in their efforts to improve their situation.

sections.paper_id: Yang et al. - 2023 - What Is “Rural Feminism” A Computer-Assisted Analysis of Popular Antifeminist Discourses in Chinese
paragraphs: Rural feminism is an offensive term that arose out of the gender debate and was initially used as a discursive weapon for men to attack female social media users, feminism, and feminists. The fact that the term is predominantly used by men is not only in line with the general subjective perception of social media users (i.e., Zhihu is often referred to as "men's Hu" to emphasize the general impression that it is dominated by male users) but also supported by relevant research data (e.g., Ma, 2020;Zhang, 2020).

According to the "Women's Curiosity" Report 4 published by China Women's Daily and Zhihu, as of 24 February 2020, the percentage of female users in Zhihu was 46%. However, in the statistics of the most influential individual users on Zhihu at different times from 2011 to the present, female users have never made it into the top five. In addition, the top-ranked female users' areas of influence usually tend to be typical female-oriented topics, such as "fashion," "skincare," "mother and baby," and "lifestyle," instead of exerting a significant impact on core topics in the traditional public sphere. For instance, in the statistics of July 2022, 5 the most influential comments made by the highest ranking female user, @倩Sur, were about "antioxidants" and "slowing down the aging process." Furthermore, according to the "Women's Curiosity" Report, feminism-related topics are not among the topics that female users are most concerned about, nor do female users demonstrate sufficient influence in this domain. A combination of reasons has thus led to the impression that Zhihu tends to be male-dominated and the base of antifeminists, even though female users are not underrepresented in the figures. In contrast, Weibo (China's equivalent of Twitter), also among the most mainstream social media platforms in China, is often seen as a platform dominated by women, or even the base of feminism (and/or pseudo-feminism).

In this vein, it is reasonable to infer men as the main producers of antifeminist discourse, which is not surprising. Scholars in China have found that not all men have been able to adapt to the changes brought about by women's progress and respond positively to women during the rise of women's status. Many men are passive and ambivalent in facing the empowerment of women and the transformation of gender role models-they are dissatisfied with the arbitrariness of the old gender order, but also reluctant to give up their vested interests, so they often unconsciously exaggerate the negative effects of disorder and misconduct in the process of gender reformation (Jin, 1996). For instance, Yefu Zheng, a male sociologist, once argued that women's emancipation pushed men into domestic work and women into society without providing them with a new model of family roles, leading to tensions within the family due to conflicting roles; whereas patriarchy, although arbitrary, provides a model that saves men and women from the confusion and conflict of individual choices (as cited in Jin, 1996). This view can be considered exemplary for the ambivalence of Chinese men in the face of women's emancipation.

The quest for gender equality is sharply divided between men and women in China: while post-1949 Chinese women of all generations generally support the ideology of gender equality and even hold expectations of gender equality that are higher-than-realistic, men's identification with the idea has continued to decline, a negative attitude that is thought to be a possible resistance by men in the face of pressure from the state and women for gender equality (Pimentel, 2006). A combination of factors has led "feminism" to be considered a long-standing pejorative term in China (e.g., Wang, 1999;Wu & Dong, 2019). This negative attitude of men toward feminism and gender equality has tended to intensify in recent years.

The core of this male discontent has always been situated in the domain of marriage and family. China's economic and social structural transformation that began in the late 1970s has shaped a new gendered power structure, on the basis of which a special "marriage market" has been constructed-that is "a heterosexual institution governed by a market logic which emphasizes women's economic dependency on men and their sexual objectification by men" (Wu & Dong, 2019, p. 18). Feminist ideas and practices in China, however, have largely challenged this "marriage market" and threatened the dominance and privilege of men in this domain. The backlash of men against feminism, in this context, is essentially a reformulation and reaffirmation of their gender hegemony (Wu & Dong, 2019). Moreover, based on China's specific historical and institutional contexts, the ever-intensifying gender antagonism is deeply entangled with class antagonism, and in a sense has even become an outlet for class conflict (Wu & Dong, 2019). It is, therefore, not difficult to understand the intensity and complexity of the gender dichotomy in China.

Recently, a number of feminist scholars have pointed out that gender inequality has resurfaced in China, and the rights and power that women were granted in previous stages have been undermined and withdrawn to some extent nowadays (e.g., Dai, 2015;Hong, 2016). The prevailing antifeminist discourses on social media are clear evidence of the reality that the gains of feminist development in China have been partially undone. In this vein, we hope to uncover the strategies of this wave of antifeminism through an examination of large-scale everyday discourses on Zhihu, the recognized stronghold of online antifeminism, to explore how this undo of feminism has been achieved, and to figure out possible responses for Chinese feminism to this backlash wave.

Rural feminism is an offensive term that arose out of the gender debate and was initially used as a discursive weapon for men to attack female social media users, feminism, and feminists. The fact that the term is predominantly used by men is not only in line with the general subjective perception of social media users (i.e., Zhihu is often referred to as "men's Hu" to emphasize the general impression that it is dominated by male users) but also supported by relevant research data (e.g., Ma, 2020;Zhang, 2020).

According to the "Women's Curiosity" Report 4 published by China Women's Daily and Zhihu, as of 24 February 2020, the percentage of female users in Zhihu was 46%. However, in the statistics of the most influential individual users on Zhihu at different times from 2011 to the present, female users have never made it into the top five. In addition, the top-ranked female users' areas of influence usually tend to be typical female-oriented topics, such as "fashion," "skincare," "mother and baby," and "lifestyle," instead of exerting a significant impact on core topics in the traditional public sphere. For instance, in the statistics of July 2022, 5 the most influential comments made by the highest ranking female user, @倩Sur, were about "antioxidants" and "slowing down the aging process." Furthermore, according to the "Women's Curiosity" Report, feminism-related topics are not among the topics that female users are most concerned about, nor do female users demonstrate sufficient influence in this domain. A combination of reasons has thus led to the impression that Zhihu tends to be male-dominated and the base of antifeminists, even though female users are not underrepresented in the figures. sections.paper_id: Yang et al. - 2023 - What Is “Rural Feminism” A Computer-Assisted Analysis of Popular Antifeminist Discourses in Chinese
Yang et al. - 2023 - What Is “Rural Feminism” A Computer-Assisted Analysis of Popular Antifeminist Discourses in Chinese
paragraphs: Based on the characteristics of our research data-huge amounts of fragmented texts (corpora) published by many social media users-we used corpus-assisted discourse studies (CADS) to achieve a comprehensive understanding of the data. Specifically, we applied latent Dirichlet allocation (LDA) topic modeling approach, as well as concordance and collocation analyses, to determine repetitive patterns of language use in our data.

CADS as a methodological innovation, which combined discourse studies with corpus linguistics (CL) research methods, has been explored and developed by many scholars with a wealth of research results (e.g., Partington, 2004;Baker et al., 2008;Jacobs & Tschötschel, 2019). Discourse analysis has traditionally been used to analyze discourses collected from mass media, not only because mass media are often considered to play a key role in the reproduction of dominant knowledge and ideologies in society and are the main channel through which elites exercise power (Van Dijk, 2005), but also because everyday discourse outside of the realm of mass media is difficult for researchers to access (Törnberg & Törnberg, 2016). This situation has now changed dramatically-with the development of Web 2.0 and especially the dramatic increase in the use of social media, a vast amount of textual data from the everyday sphere become more and more available for discourse research. The platform properties of social media, however, dictate that the textual data they provide are not only huge in quantity but also often unstructured, fragmented, and lack sufficient contextual information, which makes it difficult for the traditional qualitative and theory-based discourse analysis methods to deal with these data (Törnberg & Törnberg, 2016).

Based on these new data processing requirements, several more sophisticated models and algorithms are developed in recent years, a representative one of which is the topic modeling. Topic modeling is an umbrella term for a set of methods and algorithms that can reduce the complexity of large corpora by identifying clusters of repeated co-occurring words to discover underlying topic structures in a collection of documents (Törnberg & Törnberg, 2016;Jacobs & Tschötschel, 2019).

LDA, as the most common algorithm for performing topic modeling and the specific approach we choose for this study, is "a flexible generative probabilistic model for collections of discrete data," the core premise of which is that documents are represented as random mixes of latent topics, each of which is defined by a word distribution (Blei et al., 2003(Blei et al., , pp. 996, 1014)). Specifically, LDA is a hierarchical Bayesian model that contains three levels: documents, topics, and words. Each document implies a mixed distribution of different potential topics, and the goal of LDA is to mine the distribution patterns of words in the document in an unsupervised computational way, aggregate the words with similar distribution patterns, and extract the potential topics of the document (Blei et al., 2003). Thus, the main result obtained is the probability of each topic that each word in the corpus is assigned based on the ranking of the probability that these words represent a certain topic in the corpus (topic term matrix) (Jacobs & Tschötschel, 2019). In other words, a topic is shown as "a list of words with different assigned probabilities" (Törnberg & Törnberg, 2016, p. 405). In this vein, LDA could provide researchers with an overview, or content map, of the corpus, helping them to locate key information and core texts, thus "aiding discovery and adding analytical rigor" (Törnberg & Törnberg, 2016, p. 401).

sections.paper_id: Yang et al. - 2023 - What Is “Rural Feminism” A Computer-Assisted Analysis of Popular Antifeminist Discourses in Chinese
paragraphs: First, we used "rural feminism" as the keyword to conduct a comprehensive search of questions on Zhihu on 24 January 2021, and identified the five most answered questions related:

1. 636 answers from "Why is feminism often equated with female fist/rural feminism on the Chinese internet?" 6 2. 558 answers from "Why do some say Chinese feminism is pseudo-feminism?" 7 3. 458 answers from "What is the difference between rural feminism and authentic feminism?" 8 4. 452 answers from "What is the definition of rural feminism?" 9 5. 242 answers from "How to distinguish between feminism and rural feminism?" 10
Then, we collected all 2,346 responses to these five questions. 11 After removing repeated and nontext formatted responses, our research corpus ultimately consisted of 2,104 texts.

Second, we cleaned our dataset, including deleting stop words through an automatic segmentation program (e.g., JiebaR package), and removing gender pronouns because they appear very frequently in the documents, but do not provide valuable information for analysis and can interfere with subsequent analysis. Next, we created a document-term matrix and assigned the research dataset to 5 topics through the "Topicmodels" R package. The LDA model has two conflicting requirements for the results, namely, that (1) words from each text should appear in as few topics as possible, and (2) that the fewer words in each topic the better (Törnberg & Törnberg, 2016). The number "5" is the most consistent with these requirements and most explanatory after trying the full range of topics amounts from 2 to 10.

Moreover, normally the first 5-20 words are typically representative of a topic, depending on the criteria employed, and the topic is practically equal to this list of "top words," which can be considered as the primary resource for analyzing topics and relationships between topics (Jacobs & Tschötschel, 2019). To provide a more comprehensive understanding of each topic, we select the first 30 words to form the word list. In addition, we also applied concordance and collocation analyses to keywords identified, so as to facilitate a deeper understanding of each topic as well as the whole document (see Figure 1).

sections.paper_id: Yang et al. - 2023 - What Is “Rural Feminism” A Computer-Assisted Analysis of Popular Antifeminist Discourses in Chinese
paragraphs: Through word frequency statistics, we found that "rural feminism," "society," and "authentic feminism" are the three most frequently occurring terms. The collocation and concordance analyses of rural feminism reveals that it is most associated with the term authentic feminism. This is also consistent with the literal meaning of the five questions we collected from Zhihu-the discussion of rural feminism is largely interdependent with the discussion of authentic feminism. To be more specific, authentic feminism is often used as a comparison to rural feminism to clarify what feminist perceptions and behaviors are perceived as false, perverse, and therefore "rural." Thus, we argue that rural feminism is the more popular and common name for Chinese pseudo-feminism in the antifeminist discourse circulating on social media.

This dichotomy of rural feminism/ authentic feminism is not exceptional, as the dichotomous construction of feminism and feminists is a common attitude of men in the face of feminism, as evidenced in established studies (e.g., Edley & Wetherell, 2001;Gan, 2021). Of even greater concern are the criteria for this binary division. Through topic modeling, the dichotomy is expressed more clearly-the model presents five separate topics focusing explicitly on the comparison of rural feminism and authentic feminism from different dimensions (see Table 1). Next, we will focus on the separate topics for a more nuanced and detailed analysis.

Given that the women's liberation movement in early China followed Marxist guidance and that Marxist feminism was actually the earliest version of feminism in China, it is reasonable to confirm that the authentic feminism discussed herein refers to Marxist feminism. Moreover, by emphasizing the temporal distance between the past and the present, this authentic feminism, recognized in the antifeminist discourse, is sequestered in history and thus denied its presence in the present.
Specifically, we identified two significant strategies in the documents on this topic.

First, the existence of oppressive structures in patriarchal societies and the oppression of women are acknowledged, but these are separated from men's benefits. These discourses emphasize that men are neither the beneficiaries of patriarchal structures nor the oppressors of women; instead, they are rather victims as well. Although we see many different narratives in specific texts about how men are oppressed in the patriarchal society, they all serve the same purpose-to decouple men from the benefits and privileges of the patriarchal order. For example, 12 Both feminisms have fallen into misconceptions in current development, just as opposed to authentic feminism, rural feminism, on the other hand, is extremely one-sided and mechanical. How dare they try to take power away from men? What power do men have? . . . Men also have obligations, and the rights and responsibilities are the same. If women in the patriarchal society are victims, how are men not? Their free will is all but oppressed. It is just that men generally have more surplus value under industrial production. This has nothing to do with whether men oppress women or not. The power that men gain is never earned by oppressing women, but by their obligations. So, some feminists' attempts to take something from men are naked plunder! Naked plunder that ignores the relations of production! Unless women are the productive agents of society but are oppressed by men to extract surplus value for the benefit of men. Then some of the existing feminist demands can be called true. Authentic feminism should declare war on the production structure and relations. Break the patriarchal society where only men are the main producers. Not only will women benefit from this, but men will also benefit from it. (Example 1) Following this logic, men, like women, are objects in need of rescue from patriarchy. sections.paper_id: Yang et al. - 2023 - What Is “Rural Feminism” A Computer-Assisted Analysis of Popular Antifeminist Discourses in Chinese
sections.header_raw: Topic 2-Feminism and the Principle of Reciprocity of Rights and Duties
sections.header_norm: topic 2 feminism and the principle of reciprocity of rights and duties
paragraphs: According to the word list (see Figure 3), Topic 2 mainly measures the difference between rural feminism and authentic feminism in terms of the principle of reciprocity of rights and duties. The words that appear most in the word list are words about equality, such as "equal," "equal rights," "equality between men and women," "the same," "equivalent," and "fairness," which repeatedly suggest the central position of the category of equality. The core criterion for distinguishing between rural feminism and authentic feminism is identified as to whether the interests of men and women are treated equally.

To be more precise, if a consistent standard can be applied to men and women, and if women and men are required to assume fully equal obligations and responsibilities as a prerequisite for the equal enjoyment of rights and benefits, then it is authentic feminism that pursues equal rights, while the opposite is rural feminism that pursues women's privileges.

In fact, this authentic feminism with its emphasis on equal rights points to the liberal feminism developed in the first and second waves of Western feminism, which was the first feminist theory and practice introduced to China in the 1990s. In such a context, as opposed to this liberal authentic feminism, the highly visible feminist values and activities circulating in social media discourse, under the name of rural feminism, are accused of female privilegeism, turning into a brutal, even absurd, plundering of rights and oppression of men. For instance, Quite simply, it depends on whether she fights for obligations before she fights for rights. Many women (and men too) don't seem to understand much about the relationship between rights and obligations, and they don't understand that rights are essentially rewards for obligations. (Example 3) Whether feminism in my eyes is rural or not depends only on their attitude toward rights and obligations, and they can fight for the corresponding rights if they are willing to take on the corresponding obligations. (Example 4) Traditional society: Men have more rights and more obligations. Authentic feminism: Men are expected to transfer more rights to women, while women are willing to take on more obligations. Rural feminism: More rights, no more obligations. (Example 5)

In these discourses, rights are defined as "rewards for obligations," so that men's vested interests become their entitlements and are the rightful rewards of society for the duties they have undertaken, while the rights gained by the rural feminist movement are "transferred" from men to women. Therefore, women need to take more responsibilities while gaining rights, which are fair and equal and can be certified by men as authentic feminism. Moreover, these obligations and responsibilities, which are defined in terms of masculine standards, were often also once predominantly undertaken by men, and are naturally more in the dominant domain of the male gender. Under such a premise, women are required to fulfill obligations that are fully equal to those of men, and such standards of equality are more favorable to men as well. Thus, through these covert narrative strategies, the antifeminist discourse binds its position to the basic social consensus of equality and fundamental values of liberal feminism, thereby consolidating its legitimacy, and by defining equality in a way that is more favorable to men, it reduces the threat that the current feminist movement may pose to men's interests.

Thus, through these covert narrative strategies, the antifeminist discourse binds its position to the basic social consensus of equality and fundamental values of liberal feminism, thereby consolidating its legitimacy, and by defining equality in a way that is more favorable to men, it reduces the threat that the current feminist movement may pose to men's interests.

sections.paper_id: Yang et al. - 2023 - What Is “Rural Feminism” A Computer-Assisted Analysis of Popular Antifeminist Discourses in Chinese
sections.header_raw: Topic 3 Extreme Feminism Versus Moderate Feminism
sections.header_norm: topic 3 extreme feminism versus moderate feminism
paragraphs: Within this topic, it is notable that words such as "extreme," "radical" and "moderate," "normal," and "normal people" appear in the word list simultaneously, as well as the word "antagonism" (see Figure 4). These words echo some of the established findings-Some scholars point out that the most common pattern of perceptions of feminism, especially among men, is the dichotomous structure of extreme feminism and moderate feminism (e.g., Edley & Wetherell, 2001), and established research on rural feminism has also noted that rural feminism is often associated with radical feminist advocacy and action (e.g., Li & Gao, 2020;F. Yang, 2020;Zhang, 2020). The moderate feminism that is more readily accepted by the majority is often the feminism that demands only equality (Edley & Wetherell, 2001), and this is the authentic feminism that is endorsed by the antifeminist discourse. In other words, the "extreme/moderate" dichotomy corresponds to the rural feminism/authentic feminism distinction-feminism that merely demands equality is moderate and authentic, while feminism that demands rights beyond the boundaries of equality is denounced as extreme pseudo-feminism, that is, rural feminism. Similar to Topic 2, here again, authentic feminism points to liberal feminism with equal rights at its core.

In addition, it is worth noting that the word list for this topic includes several words about the internet, including "Zhihu," "internet," "Weibo," "on Weibo," "online," and "reality," which is often used as an antonym for the internet. Through contextual examinations, we find that "internet/ reality" constitutes another dichotomy in this topic that corresponds to the rural feminism/authentic feminism division, that is, feminism on the internet is rural and feminism in reality is authentic. This division is based on a principle that has been repeatedly emphasized and widely recognized in antifeminist discourse: authentic feminists are making real efforts to contribute to society in the real world, thus improving their status and achieving true equality, and do not have time to make eye-catching feminist statements online. In addition, beneath this layer of logic lies a more insidious dichotomy-true feminists are successful women in the real world, while rural feminists are identified as losers in the real world. Thus, feminists in social media discursive spaces are further delegitimized. For example, I know several able women in real life, who may not use Weibo, or TikTok, or may not even know the existence of Zhihu. They might be married, dinky, remarried, or divorced and insist on single parenting. I believe that no matter how they choose, they all want sweet love and harmony family, and they will improve themselves after they find that things are not what they want, and they will eventually get what they want. I think they are the most advanced representatives of feminism. They never shout slogans, do not instil their ideas on others, and rarely look down on men who are not as good as they are, let alone demand benefits from other men, they just play their roles well day in and day out. . . . I can guess that some people will tell me that they would not have had the opportunity to express themselves if it weren't for the fact that feminism in the past has given women equal access to education and employment. I don't deny that, as I described, they have played their roles well, so I'm sure it wouldn't be hard for them to deal with role changing. (Example 6)

In the logic of this discourse, successful women, as "the most advanced representatives of feminism," are capable of "playing their roles well" in any situation. In contrast, feminists online are unable to "get what they want" in the real world, so they turn to the online world to take out their grievances on men. Through this strategy, feminists active in the online discourse space are stigmatized as failed grievances, their words become the whining of losers and are thus de-emphasized. Moreover, this narrative pattern of an authentic feminist that attributes women's success to individual effort exhibits distinctly neoliberal feminist traits that encourage women to fulfill their desires and create meaningful lives through self-improvement, and take full responsibility for their achievements and life situations. This neoliberal feminism replaces feminism's focus on the foundational structures of society through its emphasis on freedom of choice, making many of feminism's foundational claims problematic, detracting from the possibility of a more feminist challenge to contemporary society and politics, and is thus noted as being largely aligned with postfeminist values (Rottenberg, 2014).

Another point of interest is that authentic feminists "never shout slogans" and "do not instil their ideas to others." Only the silent ones, who do not export their views and do not openly express their demands are the authentic feminists, while the highly visible feminists who actively express feminist demands and spread feminist ideas through the internet, especially social media, are stigmatized as rural feminists. In this way, these antifeminist discourses are trying to deprive feminists of their right to speak in cyberspace and block the publication and dissemination of feminist discourse.

sections.paper_id: Yang et al. - 2023 - What Is “Rural Feminism” A Computer-Assisted Analysis of Popular Antifeminist Discourses in Chinese
sections.header_norm: topic 4 feminism in marriage and family
paragraphs: Topic 4 (see Figure 5) is the most distinctive of the five topics, which focuses on real, concrete marriage and family matters, and differs markedly from other themes that focus on feminist issues at the macro-social level, as evidenced by the 21 unique words in the word list.

In a series of discussions about gender power relations within the family, four focal points are identified: first, "children," which is widely perceived by antifeminists as an incitement for women to objectify and sell their wombs, using fertility as a bargaining chip to blackmail men for more benefits and privileges. The second is the controversy over the division of property and the distribution of ownership between husband and wife, represented by "house," where antifeminists often accuse rural feminism of instigating women to use marriage as a means to seize property ownership and squeeze men's assets. The third is the issue of "housework," where antifeminists accuse women under the spell of rural feminism of being morally corrupt, selfish, and irresponsible for refusing to play the traditional role of a good wife and mother who is dedicated to the internal affairs of the family. The fourth focus is "bride price," one of the most distinctive feminist topics in China. Although this practice is not unique to China and has become rare in urban areas, it is still common in rural areas (Anderson, 2007). The bride price is primarily related to the threshold and cost of marriage for men, especially in rural areas-a man who cannot afford to pay the bride price cannot find a spouse, which puts him in a very difficult situation (Jiang & Sánchez-Barricarte, 2012). For example:

When an authentic feminist gets married and buys a house: we each pay half the money, and each has 50 percent of the property rights. We can negotiate when to have a child after marriage and how many children to have, but I have the final say. If we decide together to have a child, the child should be taken care of together and housework will be shared.

Authentic feminists plus true love buy a house: theoretically, each should pay half, but you have some difficulties, for the time being, so you can pay less, and I will pay more. Children and housework ditto.

Traditional women: you are the male side, my parents raised me and then gave me to your family, so your family should pay for the house. Of course, the child will have your surname, New Year's Eve and holidays we will go back to your parents' home, childcare and doing the housework are mainly on me, you need to support the family. If your parents are a little bit unreasonable, I try to hold my tongue and not talk back. Of course, if the property card can add my name, it would be better, anyway, I married you, I belong to you, as long as you do not frequent cheating or domestic violence, I will certainly not divorce.

Rural feminist: Of course, you must pay for the house, but also to write my name on the homeownership certificate, or you give birth to a child? Of course, the child should be taken care of by your mother, otherwise, will the child take my family name? Why don't your parents pay for the child's upbringing? Is this not their grandchild? What, you say you don't want a child? You deprive me of my reproductive rights! Isn't it you men's responsibility to support the family? Do the housework? Of course, you must do at least half, equality between men and women! (Example 7)

These four focal issues are of immediate interest to men and are the most concrete battlegrounds where patriarchy and feminism meet. By applying the abstract universal standard of "equality between men and women" to these concrete arenas of the struggle for gender rights to distinguish between rural feminism and authentic feminism, the antifeminist discourse is not only able to transfer the pressures on men in marriage and family to feminism and feminists but also to kidnap the authentic feminists with the universal value of equality. These women are required to do the "right" thing, that is, to play the role assigned to them by the patriarchal family structure and continue to carry the burden of longstanding tasks.

sections.header_norm: topic 5 chinese feminism versus foreign feminism
paragraphs: Within this topic (see Figure 6), the most noteworthy is a series of geocultural concepts involving China and outside of China, such as "China," "abroad," "domestic," "American," "western," "foreign countries," "our country," and "European and American." Through contextual analysis, we find that Topic 5 focuses on stating the difference between authentic feminism and rural feminism through a comparison between China and Foreign. This topic essentially presents a clearer and more concrete definition of authentic feminism as discussed in Topics 1, 2, and 3.

Specifically, there are two main strategies in the antifeminist discourse, one is to take Western liberalism feminism as the benchmark of authentic feminism and to denounce all the localized editions of feminism in China as pseudo-feminism, as discussed in Topics 2 and 3. For example, Feminism abroad is to take away the shackles from women. Chinese feminism, on the other hand, is to transfer the shackles from women to men. (Example 8) Feminism abroad is: if given the same treatment, why can't we women do what you men can do? Domestic feminism: Although given the same treatment, why should we women do as much as men? (Example 9) Another strategy in this comparative perspective of China and foreign countries is to treat the initial phase of Chinese women's liberation as authentic feminism while categorizing feminism in China today as pseudo, as discussed in Topic 1. For example:

You are wrong, Chinese feminism is authentic feminism, pseudo-feminism is spread from abroad in the past few years. Chinese feminism is the equality between men and women, and equal pay for equal work, which has been implemented since the founding of the country. Chinese feminism is "women can hold half of the sky." (Example 10) This nostalgia for the early stage of women's liberation was also a dominant voice in the antifeminist discourse, as evidenced by the phrase "women can hold up half the sky" listed in this topic, which is the core slogan of the feminist movement at the time. After the founding of the People's Republic of China in 1949, the first complete law was the Marriage Law, followed by a series of social change measures to liberate women, including abolishing arranged marriages, closing brothels, training prostitutes to reintegrate into mainstream society, and encouraging and organizing women to leave the home and participate in social affairs. Legal and institutional gender equality was guaranteed by these governmental actions. As a result, "women can hold up half the sky" became the mainstream thinking of society.

Chinese women achieved a level of equality that had never been achieved before in history, the structural and institutional gender equality that was leading the world at the time. While women tend to use this achievement as a starting point for furthering the feminist cause (Pimentel, 2006), some men, already maladjusted to previous gender reform, see feminist ideas and practices as further oppressing them. These men saw themselves as victims of increasingly "aggressive" feminist struggles, and this victimization provided the psychosocial basis for the antifeminist discourse now prevalent in social media.

sections.paper_id: Yang et al. - 2023 - What Is “Rural Feminism” A Computer-Assisted Analysis of Popular Antifeminist Discourses in Chinese
paragraphs: The above analysis revealed that rural feminism has performed as a discursive weapon, a coercive force, to punish "inappropriate" and "unfair" feminist claims (e.g., imposing higher demands on men's financial status, requiring men to take on more household chores), and to reward specific "moderate" feminism (e.g., requiring women to give up their bride price, encouraging women to shoulder more of the household expenses), so as to minimize the threat of feminism to the still largely male-dominated gender order and to protect men's vested interests. This is in line with the consistent goals of antifeminism. Furthermore, by comparing the antifeminist strategies found in this study with established analyses (both in Chinese and English venues) using various empirical approaches (e.g., Edley & Wetherell, 2001;Li & Gao, 2020;Peng, 2020;F. Yang, 2020;Zhang, 2020), we find that most of the strategies identified herein correspond to some extent to the ones that have been discussed in previous research scenarios. Our computer-assisted analyses based on a large number of texts reconfirms the salience of these strategies in the antifeminist discourse prevalent on Chinese online social media platforms.

However, some subtle but significant differences in this wave of antifeminism compared to its predecessors are worth noting. First, the antifeminist discourse that prevails on Chinese social media platforms frequently originates from circumstances of mutual aggressiveness among social media users, particularly in confrontations between male and female users. As a result, it is typically manifested in discursive attacks and online violence directed at specific female users, exhibiting a distinctly misogynistic tendency. This amalgamation with misogyny could well have reinforced the destructive and aggressive nature of this wave of antifeminism.

Moreover, the absence of a core discourse strategy, that is, the devaluation of feminists' sexual attractiveness, is even more noteworthy. As an important means for men to counteract feminist transgressions, this strategy has been repeatedly demonstrated in previous studies (e.g., Wu & Dong, 2019). As discussed earlier, the realm of marriage and family has always been a central concern of gender antagonism in China, and a core reason for the male backlash against feminism is exactly that it directly challenges male gender privilege in the "marriage market." Some men see themselves as the "buyers" in this market and women as commodities. In their logic, women would be worthless without their sexual value, so devaluing the sexual attractiveness of women is generally seen as a very powerful weapon (Wu & Dong, 2019). This strategy, however, according to our statistics, is used on a much smaller scale to the extent that it is no longer prominent in the larger-scale statistical results.

Much of this may be due to the fact that, with the popularization of feminism in China, the basic conceptions of feminism have become more widespread, including the perception of misogyny and objectification of women. And men's devaluation of women's sexual attractiveness, however, often manifests itself in the humiliation of women's looks and figures, displaying obvious misogyny and objectification of women, which have been repeatedly exposed and criticized by feminist discussions in social media. This positive change implies that this wave of antifeminists is not wholly negative about feminism and that they may also be open to the influence of certain feminist values. This is not difficult to understand, given that it is rooted in the same communicative environment with popular feminism, subject to the same logic of media capitalism, and encouraged and constrained by the same media algorithm and other background conditions to compete for visibility and the attention of the same potential user group. Existed studies have examined how Chinese social media influencers intentionally employ various strategies to promote male pro-feminist participation (e.g., X. Yang et al., 2022). Similarly, antifeminists active on social media platforms have also adopted various strategies to bargain with feminism and gain acceptance of their antifeminist goals from the general public, not just men, by promoting universally accepted values such as equality, fairness, and Marxism. The words that indicate these values can be found in the top keywords list of the five topics.

In fact, social media antifeminists have largely positioned themselves as defenders of authentic feminism rather than challengers. They seem convinced that their attacks on pseudo-feminism (i.e., rural feminism) are in defense of true feminist values. This self-perception can be inferred not only through the dichotomy of authentic feminism/pseudo-feminism, but also through the repeated invocation of particular feminist values and practices in these antifeminist discourses.

Specifically, among the multiple and diverse versions of feminism that existed in China, antifeminists have chosen Western liberal feminism and the early version of socialist feminism as the true feminist values and practices. On the basis of this strategy, which we call a "double embrace," antifeminism also achieved what we call a "double rejection" strategy, that is, the rejection and devaluation of contemporary, localized feminism and feminists. This strategy of "double embrace" and "double rejection" has been repeatedly confirmed in the above analyses of antifeminist discourse. Both of the so-called authentic versions of feminism embraced by antifeminism have long been marginalized in the current feminist landscape in China (F. Yang, 2020). Moreover, since they are temporally and spatially detached from the current Chinese gender reality, that is, they have a significant nonlocal and noncurrent quality, which makes them incapable of making a concrete and realistic critique of the current Chinese social reality, and therefore incapable of posing a serious threat to men's vested interests. The versions of feminism that have suffered a "double rejection," on the other hand, cover feminist ideologies and practices that are alive and well in the current Chinese society and capable of exerting a powerful influence on the still largely maledominated gender order.

This strategy synthesis of "double embrace" and "double rejection" also hints that this wave of backlash against feminism popular on social media platforms aims to exploit the complex history and reality of women's liberation in China, identifies contradictions and differences between different versions of Chinese feminism, and embeds itself within that system. This allows it not only to grow concealed under the cover of feminist values but also divides and undermines the emancipatory power of feminism from within. This concealment has enabled it to gain the support of the public, even including women, on social media as a feminist sympathizer and supporter, rather than a saboteur.

sections.paper_id: Yang et al. - 2023 - What Is “Rural Feminism” A Computer-Assisted Analysis of Popular Antifeminist Discourses in Chinese
paragraphs: In conclusion, the antifeminist wave in Chinese social media, marked most notably by the stigmatization buzzword "rural feminism," employed strategies that we defined as "double embrace" and "double rejection" to minimize the realistic threat of feminism to the current gender order and male interests in China. These deliberate strategies hint at and further exacerbate the complex interplay between current Chinese antifeminism and feminism. This means that the previous framework of understanding this relationship as a simple dichotomy no longer applies, and that we need to reconceptualize the current challenges and threats to Chinese feminism in the context of the specific history and reality of Chinese society and the rich interactions between antifeminism and misogyny, neoliberalism, postfeminism, and various versions of feminism active in China.
